text: Reasoning is a fundamental aspect of human intelligence, and is central to tasks such as solving complex problems, making intelligent decisions, and drawing conclusions from given information. Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have started to learn and become more knowledgeable, and it is becoming increasingly clear that the newer and more advanced LLMs learn to reason as well. But to what extent are they actually able to do so? This paper gives a summary of the state of the art in reasoning in LLMs. It describes the various methods to extract and improve the logical abilities of LLMs, reviews the datasets and metrics used to assess them, and summarizes the recent findings of the empirical research made in this field. We discuss the implications of the advancement in this field and highlight the missing pieces that need to be filled, and thus, suggest some directions for future work. Our goal is to propose a summary and state of the art of the field and spark further discussions and research on the AI and cognitive computing front.
